title: Korean Vegetable Mandu

overview.description: Korean dumplings filled with tofu, glass noodles, cabbage, mushrooms and vegetables, wrapped in thin dumpling wrappers.

ingredients: 200g napa cabbage or white cabbage, finely chopped
method: Crumble the tofu into a clean tea towel or muslin cloth.
Squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible.
Cook the glass noodles according to the packet instructions.
Drain thoroughly and cut into short lengths.
Place the chopped cabbage in a bowl.
Sprinkle with a little salt and leave for 10 minutes.
Squeeze the cabbage firmly to remove excess liquid.
Heat a small amount of oil in a frying pan.
Cook the mushrooms until their moisture has evaporated.
Add the garlic and ginger and cook for 1 minute.
Allow the mushrooms to cool.
Place the tofu, glass noodles, cabbage, carrots, mushrooms and spring onions in a large bowl.
Add the soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar and black pepper.
Add the beaten egg if using.
Mix thoroughly.
Place a wrapper in the palm of your hand.
Add approximately 1 tablespoon of filling to the centre.
Moisten the edge of the wrapper with water.
Fold the wrapper over the filling and press firmly to seal.
Pleat the edge if desired.
Repeat with the remaining wrappers and filling.
To cook immediately, pan-fry the mandu in a little oil until golden underneath.
Add a splash of water to the pan and cover immediately.
Steam until the wrappers are tender and the filling is hot.
Alternatively, steam the mandu until cooked through.

learning.did-you-know: Mandu are Korean dumplings that can be steamed, boiled, pan-fried or added to soups, making them one of the most versatile foods in Korean cooking.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare a dumpling filling with controlled moisture and how to fold and cook dumplings without the wrappers becoming soggy.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A freezer-friendly vegetarian snack that is easy to batch cook and provides a different texture and flavour profile from the Indian fritters already in the collection.
chapman-tips: Removing moisture from the tofu and cabbage is the most important step.
Cook the mushrooms until their water has evaporated.
Keep unused wrappers covered with a damp tea towel so they do not dry out.
Freeze the mandu in a single layer before transferring them to a freezer bag.
Cook from frozen rather than thawing if possible.
Frozen mandu may need a few extra minutes of steaming.
The egg helps bind the filling but can be omitted for a slightly looser, more traditional-style filling.
A food processor can make preparing the vegetables much quicker, but avoid turning them into a wet paste.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes extremely well. Freeze uncooked mandu in a single layer until solid, then transfer to bags or containers.
notes.leftovers: Cooked mandu can be refrigerated and reheated, although freshly cooked dumplings have the best texture.
